title: When CAN you pay a Chinese supplier by card?

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers, your credit card is not useless. It's just been mis-sold to you. Here's the math. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. So 95% of factories will say no. But there are three places where cards actually win. One. Samples and first orders under 1,000 dollars. Card chargeback gives you a 180-day window to recover bad goods. T/T cannot do that. Two. Alibaba Trade Assurance orders up to 12,000 dollars. Alibaba escrows the money so the supplier accepts the card. Three. Through a sourcing agent who takes your card and settles to the factory in CNY. Outside those three, cards fail. The 2.95% processing fee and 180-day chargeback risk make every factory say no. Stop forcing cards on bulk production. Use cards where they win. Use a CNY rail like WorldFirst where they don't. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Cold open + the $12K wall
scripts.original_vo: Watch this. There's a 12,000 dollar wall on every credit card payment to a Chinese supplier and most business owners hit it without knowing why. Alibaba Trade Assurance caps card payments at 12K per order. Why? Because above that, the 180-day chargeback risk on one order is too big for Alibaba to escrow against the supplier. So the system pushes you to T/T. 12K is roughly the line where samples end and production begins. Cards live below the wall. Production lives above it. Same factory. Different rail. The mistake is thinking you can scale a 500-dollar sample order to a 50,000 dollar production run on the same card. You can't. The factory will refuse. The 2.95% surcharge will bleed your margin. The chargeback risk will scare the supplier. Listen. Cards for samples. CNY rails for production. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, no 12K ceiling. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Accusation opener + reframe
scripts.original_vo: You've been blaming your supplier for refusing your card and it's the wrong target. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay domestically. They've never set up a Visa terminal. They never will. And even if they did, every card payment gives you 180 days to chargeback on goods that already shipped. Your supplier isn't refusing your card because they hate you. They're refusing because cards were built for retail buyers, not factory orders. Stop. The three places where cards actually work. Samples under 1,000 dollars where the 2.95% fee is small and the chargeback protects you. Trade Assurance orders up to 12K where Alibaba holds the dispute risk. Sourcing agents who absorb the card and settle in CNY. Outside those, T/T or a CNY rail. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Same-day. Full amount. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they try to pay a Chinese supplier by card. The card is not the fee. The card is the risk. You see a 2.95% processing fee on Alibaba. You think that's the cost. Wrong. The real cost is the 180-day chargeback window. From the supplier's side, every card payment is a 6-month loan with a return policy. They ship the goods. You get the goods. You file a chargeback. The supplier loses the money and the inventory. That's why 95% of factories refuse cards. It's not the fee they're scared of. It's the clawback. Trade Assurance solves it up to 12K because Alibaba holds the escrow. Above 12K, no escrow, no card. That's the wall. Stop. Cards work in three places. Samples. Trade Assurance under 12K. Sourcing agents. Outside those, you need a CNY rail. WorldFirst sends CNY direct, same-day, full amount, no chargeback risk for your supplier. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Direct address + use-case stack
scripts.original_vo: If you're paying Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your credit card has exactly three jobs and you're using it for the wrong ones. Job one. Samples under 1,000 dollars. The 2.95% fee is 30 bucks. The 180-day chargeback covers you if the sample is bad. Use the card. Job two. Trade Assurance on Alibaba up to 12K. Alibaba escrows the money. The supplier accepts the card because the platform holds the dispute risk. Use the card. Job three. Sourcing agents who take the card on their side and settle to the factory in CNY. Agent takes a 3 to 5% bridge fee. Sometimes worth it. Outside those three, cards fail. The factory says no. You waste 2 days. You scramble for T/T. Stop using the card for jobs four, five and six. Use a CNY rail. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, no 12K ceiling, no chargeback risk. If you're paying suppliers in China, you need both. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.original_vo: Stop. Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You keep trying to pay your supplier with a credit card. They keep saying no. You think they're being difficult. They're not. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. And every card payment gives you 180 days to chargeback. scripts.subtitle: I'll say it opinion-as-truth
scripts.original_vo: I'll say it. The credit card is the most over-recommended payment method in the cross-border space and most business owners are using it for the wrong job. You think because a card works on Alibaba, it works for every Chinese supplier. It doesn't. 95% of factories never set up a card terminal. And the 2.95% processing fee plus 180-day chargeback risk is why. But here's where cards actually win. Samples under 1,000 dollars. Trade Assurance orders up to 12,000 dollars. Through a sourcing agent. Three jobs. That's it. For everything else, the right rail is direct CNY. WorldFirst sends CNY to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, no 12K ceiling, no chargeback risk for your supplier. The card is a tool. It's just not every tool. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Stop fighting / right-tool reframe
scripts.original_vo: Stop fighting your Chinese supplier on the credit card. I'm serious. The card is a tool. It's the wrong tool for production orders. You wouldn't use a screwdriver to drive a nail. So why are you trying to pay a 50,000 dollar bulk order with a Visa? Here's what's actually happening. Only 3 to 5% of factories accept cards. The other 95% run on Alipay and WeChat Pay. And the 180-day chargeback window is poison to a supplier who already shipped the goods. They'll just say no. But cards work for samples under 1,000 dollars. They work for Trade Assurance up to 12K. They work through sourcing agents. Right job, right tool. Outside those, use a CNY rail. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. Full amount. No 12K ceiling. Match the tool to the job. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you / supplier-side reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The reason your factory keeps saying no isn't the 2.95% processing fee. It's the 180-day chargeback. Every card payment is a 6-month window where you, the buyer, can pull the money back. The goods already shipped. The factory already paid the workers. And you have half a year to dispute. From the supplier's side, that's not a payment. That's a loan with a return policy. That's why 95% of Chinese factories refuse cards on production orders. Trade Assurance fixes it up to 12K because Alibaba holds the escrow. Samples are fine because the value is small. Sourcing agents absorb it because that's their job. Outside those three, cards fail. The fix is a CNY rail. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Final, same-day, no chargeback hanging over your supplier. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + the WhatsApp scenario
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You message your supplier on WhatsApp. Can I pay by Visa? They say no. You ask why. They give you some answer about fees. You don't believe them. You ask another supplier. Same answer. You think the whole supply chain is gaslighting you. They're not. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept cards. The other 95% never set up a card terminal because they run on Alipay and WeChat Pay domestically. And the 180-day chargeback window scares every single one of them on any order above sample size. The card has three jobs. Samples under 1,000 dollars. Trade Assurance up to 12K. Sourcing agents. That's the whole list. I see this every week. Operators forcing cards on production runs, suppliers refusing, two days lost. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No card surcharge. No chargeback. Search WorldFirst. Now.

PH1.analysis: Core teaching is defensible. The 3 to 5% acceptance figure aligns with the card-acceptance reality in China B2B. The three use cases (samples, Trade Assurance, sourcing agent) are technically accurate. The 12K Trade Assurance cap and 2.95% fee are real Alibaba numbers. Main red flag is presenting the 3 to 5% as a fixed range without sourcing, and 'every factory will say no' on production orders is an absolute that any Malaysian SMB with a long-standing supplier relationship will challenge. 'T/T cannot do that' on chargeback is technically true but a skeptic might note they've never needed chargeback because they trust their factory.

skeptic_comments.text: 3 to 5% accept cards? My supplier in Yiwu take card no problem. Maybe just my factory.
180 days chargeback I never use lah. I trust my supplier already 5 years.
Sourcing agent 3 to 5% fee? Some take 10%. Number feel low.
Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not?
Sound like ad la, but at least you say card got use also. Not pure trash card.

PH2.analysis: The 12K wall reasoning (chargeback risk too big for Alibaba to escrow) is plausible but presented as definitive when Alibaba's actual cap-setting logic isn't publicly disclosed. The samples-vs-production framing is clean and useful. 'No 12K ceiling' on WorldFirst is technically true for CNY direct but a careful skeptic will ask about WF's own per-transaction limits, KYC thresholds, and whether 'same-day' applies to all amounts. 'Same factory, different rail' is a good teaching beat but slightly oversimplified because not every factory has both card and T/T workflows ready.

skeptic_comments.text: 12K wall because of chargeback risk, says who? You make up the reason or got source?
WorldFirst no ceiling? Sure or not? Bigger amount sure need extra KYC.
Same factory different rail, ok this part make sense actually.
Same day every amount? Even 100K? I doubt.
Production starts at 12K? My factory MOQ already 30K lah, no relation.

PH3.analysis: The 'blaming your supplier' frame is sharp and resonant. The 3 to 5% acceptance figure repeats from PH1, same defensibility. 'Never set up a Visa terminal. They never will.' is a sweeping absolute that any skeptic with a tier-1 export factory contact can disprove because some larger Chinese exporters have set up card facilities for international clients. 'Goods that already shipped' framing on chargeback is a real supplier concern but doesn't apply to deposit-stage payments. Sourcing agent settling in CNY is accurate but glosses over the agent fee and FX margin on the agent side.

skeptic_comments.text: Never set up Visa terminal, never will? Some big factories accept Amex now actually.
Chargeback on goods that shipped, but deposit payment supplier got nothing to lose what.
Sourcing agent settle in CNY, but agent also got their own FX margin right? Hidden cost again.
Ya the blame supplier part funny. Most operators I know do blame the factory.
Who regulate WorldFirst in my country? Need to check.

PH4.analysis: The 'card is not the fee, card is the risk' reframe is the strongest teaching in the set and largely defensible. The 180-day chargeback mechanism is real and a genuine supplier concern. '6-month loan with a return policy' is metaphor not claim, fine. 'No chargeback risk for your supplier' on WF side is technically accurate because CNY direct rails don't have card chargeback mechanics, but a skeptic could note that T/T also has no chargeback so this isn't unique to WF. Main red flag is implying WF solves a problem that any T/T solution also solves.

skeptic_comments.text: No chargeback risk on CNY direct, ok. But my T/T from bank also no chargeback. So what's the WF angle?
Card is risk not fee, ok this part actually make me think. Good frame.
6 month loan with return policy, that's a good line. Will steal it for my supplier chat.
Above 12K no escrow no card, true. But Alibaba sometimes raise the cap for trusted buyers, not always 12K.
Same day full amount, sure or not. Want to see real WF receipt screenshot.

PH5.analysis: Cleanest Hormozi script in the set. The three-jobs framing is concrete and falsifiable. Specific numbers (30 bucks for 2.95% on 1K, 3 to 5% agent fee, 2 days lost) are realistic and well-bounded. 'Use a CNY rail' as the close is honest because it positions WF as complementary to cards, not replacement. The 'you need both' close was wisely softened to 'WorldFirst is worth a look' in the iterated version. Main residual risk is the 2 days lost figure which is averaged and might feel low to someone who's been stuck longer.

skeptic_comments.text: Three jobs for the card, ok this is the cleanest version. Actually useful.
2 days lost when factory refuse? More like 5 days for me, plus weekend.
Agent 3 to 5% fee, fair. Some take more but in range.
Need both, ok. But who is WorldFirst leh. Brand recognition zero for me.
Worth a look close is softer. Less salesy than other ones. Like this one more.

PG1.analysis: The supplier-isn't-being-difficult frame is empathy-led and lands well. Same 3 to 5% acceptance figure as Hormozi set, same defensibility profile. '6-month loan with a return policy' is a clean metaphor. 'They will not accept that on a 50K production order' is absolute when in practice some suppliers will accept cards on Trade Assurance for amounts up to the 12K cap (which the script also acknowledges, creating a small internal inconsistency on the 50K example). 'No fighting the card. Match the rail to the job.' close is genuinely useful framing.

skeptic_comments.text: Supplier not being difficult, ok thanks for not blaming me at least.
Will not accept on 50K, but you said Trade Assurance up to 12K accept card. So 50K is the issue not card itself?
Match the rail to the job, that's actually a good line.
Who is WorldFirst? First time hearing. Got app?
Same day to Chinese accounts, every bank? ICBC, BoC, Construction Bank all same day?

PG2.analysis: 'Most over-recommended payment method' is opinion framed as opinion ('I'll say it' / 'in the cross-border space'), defensible Gary Vee voice. The three-jobs framing is clean. 'You think because a card works on Alibaba, it works for every Chinese supplier' is a real misconception in the SMB importer space and a strong observation. 'The card is a tool. It's just not every tool.' close is among the most balanced in the set, doesn't overpromise WF. Most defensible Gary Vee script for a skeptical Malaysian SMB audience.

skeptic_comments.text: Over-recommended payment, fair. Influencers always say credit card for points.
Card not every tool, ok. Balanced view. Less salesy than the others.
WorldFirst same-day, no ceiling, no chargeback. Sound too good. What's the catch?
3 to 5% acceptance, where you source this number from? Statista? Alibaba report?
Like this one. Not bashing card, just placing it. More credible.

PG3.analysis: The screwdriver-nail metaphor is clean. Same 3 to 5% and 95% figures as before, same risk profile. 'Poison to a supplier who already shipped the goods' is dramatic but accurate framing for the chargeback concern on production orders, though again doesn't apply to deposit-stage card payments. 'No 12K ceiling' on WF side is accurate but again a skeptic will ask about WF-specific limits. The right-job-right-tool close is one of the better positioning beats in the set. Main red flag is the 50,000 dollar example combined with 'just say no' when Alibaba Trade Assurance does enable cards up to 12K.

skeptic_comments.text: Screwdriver nail analogy ok but show me WorldFirst actual flow first.
50K production with Visa, ya factory just refuse outright. True observation.
Poison to supplier, bit dramatic. Most factories just decline, not poisoned.
No 12K ceiling, but does WF have own ceiling? Per transaction? Per month?
WorldFirst is Ant Group right? Or different company? Need to check.

PG4.analysis: Supplier-side reveal is the strongest insight in the script. The 'workers already paid, goods already shipped, you have 180 days to dispute' walkthrough is mechanically accurate and explains supplier hesitation cleanly. 'Trade Assurance fixes it up to 12K, samples are fine because value is small, sourcing agents absorb it because that's their job' is a clean three-part justification. Iterated version softening 'truth nobody's telling you' to 'something nobody's telling you' reduces conspiratorial tone well. 'Final, same-day, no chargeback hanging over your supplier' is a confident WF claim but defensible because CNY rails genuinely don't carry card-network chargeback mechanics.

skeptic_comments.text: Supplier side view actually new for me. I always think from buyer side only. Ok point taken.
180 days dispute on goods shipped, that's real. Saw a guy do that on Reddit.
Sourcing agent absorb it because that's their job, ok but they charge for it. Not free.
Final, no chargeback, ok. But T/T from my bank also final. So WF advantage is the rate not the finality?
Who is WorldFirst, Malaysia license, need to research before I touch.

PG5.analysis: The WhatsApp scenario is the most concretely SMB-relatable beat in the entire set. Every Malaysian SMB importer has had this exact conversation. 'You think the whole supply chain is gaslighting you' is a great recognition line. The three jobs are listed cleanly. 'Two days lost' is realistic. Main residual risk is 'every single one of them on any order above sample size' which is the universal-claim trap from Hormozi PH3 in slightly softer Gary Vee form. Overall the most defensible script in the Gary Vee set because it's anchored in shared experience rather than just numbers.

skeptic_comments.text: WhatsApp scenario haha exactly. Same conversation 4 times last month.
Gaslighting you, ya I really thought my suppliers all coordinating against me lol.
Every single one, above sample size? Some accept on Trade Assurance lah. Not absolute.
Two days lost, more like one week when you also waiting for bank to set up T/T.
Ok this one feel real. But still need to check WorldFirst regulator and reviews.
